posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
m. ristev:

w-s low is never an accurate price gauge especially for allocated wines. merchants who charge very fair prices will never post that stuff online

+1. And very much the case with DRC. Sure there are secondary market buyers, but at this point the market is so hot that the real fight is to get on the allocations list with your local retailer (or to get on the “special list” with non-flip provisions). Then once you get your handful of bottles and have something in hand, the secondary market prices are suddenly far less attractive.

These are decent prices on bottles that are non-existent in the open marketplace for all practical purposes- a bit above standard markup at true release price, but that could be because the retailer had to pay a premium for leftovers- but there is not a good flip opportunity here.

posts.name: Bill Tex Landreth
posts.cooked: They have 2000, 2002 and 2004 Grange. That is a little head start in front of DRC.

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
Bill Tex Landreth:

Not interested in flipping. My concern is I am not a spring chicken and I drink wines way too young.

Don’t know if I REALLY want/can wait 20+ years.

20 years is not necessary for the Echezeaux in my opinion. The 1993 is about the biggest and most backward one I ever tasted and it was drinking beautifully at age 10. Grand Echezeaux is another matter.

Here is the thing about DRC- the notion that every bottle needs 20+ years is a fairly new one, and a lot of that is built off TNs from Rudy bottles.

25 years ago when I first got into wine, DRC over about 30 years old actually started to go down in value at auction. In the late 1990s, the 1985s were typically the high point at auction, and sometimes the 1978s. But a friend of mine who is the most astute DRC collector I have ever met once told me- over a glass of 1959 Grands Echezeaux from his cellar (a vintage he felt merited the wait)- that he drank his 78s by the time they were 20 because they were fully ready.

Now, I do think that went a bit too far in the other direction- but the fact remains DRC, great as they are, do not have some magical need to age for longer than other top Burgundies. Romanee-Conti and maybe Grands-Echezeaux are the only ones where I think the 20 year rule is a good one to follow for the most part.

The real trick is this- these are highly nuanced wines and meant to be ageworthy. So there is something of a gamble since you really need a good decade to see where the vintage is going. The reason I never buy unicorns anymore at these price points (which is the only choice in many cases with allocations so tight) is that unless you have 3-4 bottles, it is a pure gamble if you are going to open one at the right time.

If you really want to have at least one- go for the Echezeaux. That one shuts down, but rarely to the degree of the others, and I have never had one that did not give at least some pleasure regardless of age. That is the safest bet of the bunch for opening at any age- and quite frankly it is one of my favorite DRCs. Grands Echezeaux can take a terribly long time, and if you open it too soon then it can be almost impossible to enjoy. That one shuts down really hard when it shuts down. Vintage is another reason to choose the Echezeaux IMHO.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
I have to dissent. While some DRC vintages show well sooner than age 20, the structure, oak, and intensity cry out for aging. Tomorrow I am opening a 96 La Tâche and know it is way too young. But it’s an LT dinner. 2000 will steal the show.

Ech does open sooner than GE but my opinion is they are usually drunk way too young.

Allen Meadows says in its book “The pearl of the Cote” that his lifelong fascination with Burgundy started with a bottle of DRC Richebourg. A 1967 drunk in 1978. It was an epiphany to him and the best wine he had ever drunk (at the time), and what pushed him to go to Burgundy for the first time.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
The prices of these wines may be relativ interesting when compared to the somewhat absurd tariffs we experienced lately. If you are interested to know what a DRC, Grange or Montebello is about and you have lots of cash it is an opportunity. If you are interested in drinking fine Burgundy from Vosne Romanee, Syrah or Cabernet its not necessary to spend so much money. Pretty similar tasting experiences can be had to a fraction of those prices.

The truth is: DRC, Grange and other rare wines get so much attention that one may think these wines are absolutely unique and no other drop can replace the otherworldly experience. Therefore the price is justified. But that’s nothing but a myth and imagination. That is my conviction after tasting good vintages of La Tache, Grange etc. These wines are really top. But certainly not miles ahead of anything else.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
A couple of years ago, we did several major Burgundy verticals. Ponsot and Dujac Clos de la Roche and Clos Saint Denis and DRC RSV. Most of the wines went back to the seventies, and two things were clear. Anything less than twenty years old was interesting but not pleasurable, and if the wines survived, and almost all had, the older ones were the stars, much more interesting, and gave much more pleasure. For instance the 1999 Ponsot Clos de la Roche was an absolute cracker but given the concentration and quality, it was at least twenty years from maturity. Things only began to get interesting with the 1993.

If Bill is interesting in drinking the Grands Ech, and twenty plus years of waiting is too long, don’t buy them. He can no doubt flip them, make some money but that was not his question.

Alan- bring the 95 instead.Last time I tasted them side by side , it ran away from the 96.
Bill- Definitely by the Echezeaux. Drinks the youngest of the entire line up.Can be singing after as little as 8 yrs,but normal in the 10-13 yr window. 2000 was the most precocious , and the 99 is just starting to open, depending on storage. Also, with the great producers, 2014 is awesome.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked:
Bill,
It sure seems like these prices (while good or at least representative of the market) are outside your comfort zone. Otherwise you wouldn’t frame your decision as a “gamble.” I personally try not to purchase wines at prices outside my comfort zone because 1) I don’t want to nervous about opening at an opportune time and 2) I don’t want to be miserable if the wine performs below (perhaps artificially high) expectations. You have an additional issue in that you may not be willing to exhibit the patience necessary for these wines to show their best.

So I would recommend that you don’t buy them, on GENERAL principle. Please note that I have NO expertise or experience with DRC wines. I don’t buy or drink them. I’d love to drink them, but I wouldn’t feel right purchasing them. Note that it’s about personal comfort, not just affordability. I don’t at all begrudge the folks on the board who can afford the wines AND are comfortable spending those dollars. I try also not to be jealous. I do occasionally live vicariously though through TNs.

Regards,
Peter
